methodology: Ratification reach for rights-expansion and protection treaties. Number of state parties reflects status from the UN Treaty Collection. The most-ratified treaties show what humanity has formally agreed on, even where compliance falls short.
data_status: illustrative — reflects public ratification status as of last verification

treaties.name: Geneva Conventions (I–IV)
treaties.scope: Laws of war; protection of wounded, sick, prisoners, civilians
treaties.notes: Universal ratification. Compliance is uneven; the standard itself is the most-agreed-upon body of conduct rules in history. Additional Protocols (1977, 2005) have fewer parties.

treaties.name: Convention on the Rights of the Child
treaties.scope: Most widely ratified human-rights treaty in history
treaties.notes: Every UN member state except the United States has ratified. Establishes a child-rights floor governments can be measured against.

treaties.name: Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer
treaties.scope: First universally ratified UN treaty; phase-out of CFCs and HCFCs
treaties.notes: Ozone layer projected to recover to 1980 levels by 2066 over most of the globe. Kigali Amendment (2016) added HFCs.

treaties.name: Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women
treaties.notes: Near-universal but with substantive reservations from many state parties. Reporting cycle is the active accountability mechanism.

treaties.name: International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination
treaties.scope: Prohibits racial discrimination in all its forms
treaties.notes: One of the earliest core UN rights treaties; Article 4 hate-speech provisions remain contested in some Western jurisdictions.

treaties.name: WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control
treaties.scope: First treaty negotiated under WHO; tobacco demand- and supply-reduction measures
treaties.notes: Credited with rapid global declines in adult smoking prevalence; structural rather than behavioral intervention.

treaties.name: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES)
treaties.scope: Regulates international trade in wildlife to prevent overexploitation
treaties.notes: Three appendix tiers (I, II, III) by threat level. Has measurably reduced commercial trade in many flagship species; enforcement uneven on harder-to-monitor items (timber, marine, parts).

treaties.name: Minamata Convention on Mercury
treaties.scope: Protects human health and environment from anthropogenic mercury
treaties.notes: Phase-out timetable for many mercury products; small-scale gold mining is the harder unfinished file.

treaties.name: Marrakesh Treaty (accessible books)
treaties.scope: Mandatory copyright exceptions for blind, visually impaired, and print-disabled readers
treaties.notes: Pioneered the principle that copyright cannot block access for the print-disabled. Cross-border exchange of accessible books is now legal.

treaties.name: Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework
treaties.scope: 30% of land and ocean protected by 2030; restoration of degraded ecosystems
treaties.notes: Adopted under the Convention on Biological Diversity (which itself has 196 parties; US is the principal non-party). Implementation is the next test.

treaties.name: Ramsar Convention on Wetlands
treaties.scope: Designation and protection of wetlands of international importance
treaties.notes: Over 2,500 Ramsar sites covering ~2.5M km². The oldest of the modern environmental treaties still in force.

treaties.name: UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)
treaties.scope: The constitution of the oceans — sovereignty, EEZs, freedom of navigation, deep-sea mining regime
treaties.notes: US is the largest non-party (signed but not ratified). The 2023 BBNJ ('high seas') agreement adds a biodiversity protection layer; entry into force pending.

treaties.name: Outer Space Treaty
treaties.scope: No national appropriation of celestial bodies; no WMDs in orbit; rescue obligations
treaties.notes: All major space-faring states are parties. Foundational regime for orbital governance; new commercial actors are testing the edges. Newer space treaties (Moon Agreement) have far fewer parties.

treaties.name: Antarctic Treaty
treaties.scope: Continent reserved for peace and science; no military activity; mineral mining banned (Madrid Protocol 1998)
treaties.notes: One of the most successful arms-control + environmental governance regimes ever signed. Parties include all 12 original signatories plus 44 acceding states; consultative status (decision-making) is more restricted.

treaties.name: International Health Regulations (2005)
treaties.scope: Mandatory reporting of public-health emergencies; core capacity requirements; declaration of PHEIC
treaties.notes: Near-universal among WHO member states. 2024 amendments tightened reporting timelines and clarified equitable access provisions during pandemics.

treaties.name: Paris Agreement (UNFCCC)
treaties.scope: Hold warming well below 2°C; pursue 1.5°C; nationally determined contributions; transparency framework
treaties.notes: Near-universal ratification. Implementation gap is the open accountability question. The Global Stocktake mechanism formalizes 5-yr review cycles.

treaties.name: ILO Core Labour Conventions (8 conventions)
treaties.scope: Freedom of association, collective bargaining, abolition of forced labor, abolition of child labor, non-discrimination
treaties.notes: Average ratification across the 8 core conventions; some (C087 on freedom of association) are widely ratified, others (C190 on violence and harassment) more slowly. ILO Declaration on Fundamental Principles applies to ILO members regardless of ratification.

treaties.name: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities
treaties.scope: First UN human-rights treaty of the 21st century; comprehensive disability-rights framework
treaties.notes: Near-universal but US signed (2009) without Senate ratification. Optional Protocol (individual communications) has fewer parties.
